fixes.scenario: You are the technical lead for a new autonomous drone project. The drone uses a low-cost Cortex-M7 microcontroller (1MB Flash, 256KB SRAM) for its flight controller. A new requirement has come down: add a 'situational awareness' feature that fuses data from three high-frequency sensors (IMU, barometer, microphone) in real-time to predict flight instability. The research team has proposed a small, transformer-like model with 16 layers, embedding dimension 128, using FP16 precision. It requires a 512-token KV-cache to maintain temporal context. Your constraints are a hard real-time deadline of 1ms per inference and the existing MCU. The junior team's initial analysis concludes it's impossible, as the KV-cache alone (2 * 512 tokens * 16 layers * 128 embed_dim * 2 bytes = 4 MB) is 16x larger than the available SRAM. Design a system architecture that makes this model feasible. What are your first three architectural decisions, and justify them with quantitative analysis?
fixes.napkin_math: 1. **Verify Impossibility of Naive Approach:** KV-cache = 2 (K+V) * 512 tokens * 16 layers * 128 embed_dim * 2 bytes (FP16) = 4,194,304 bytes = 4 MB. Available SRAM = 256 KB. Ratio: 4 MB / 256 KB = 16x oversubscribed. Even INT8 quantization of the cache (halving to 2 MB) still leaves an 8x gap. 2. **Streaming/Sliding Window:** Use a sliding window of 64 tokens instead of 512, reducing KV-cache to 2 * 64 * 16 * 128 * 2 = 512 KB. Still 2x SRAM. 3. **Layer-by-layer DMA Tiling:** Only keep 1 layer's KV-cache in SRAM at a time: 2 * 64 * 128 * 2 = 32 KB per layer. Stream remaining layers from Flash via DMA double-buffering. SRAM budget: 32 KB active + 32 KB prefetch + ~64 KB activations + ~128 KB weights = ~256 KB. Fits within SRAM. 4. **Latency Check:** Flash read at 100 MB/s for 32 KB = 0.32 ms per layer. 16 layers = 5.12 ms. Exceeds 1ms deadline. Must reduce to 4 layers or use INT8 cache (16 KB/layer, 0.16 ms/layer, 4 layers = 0.64 ms). Feasible with architectural co-design.
fixes.realistic_solution: The problem requires abandoning the monolithic 'all-in-SRAM' memory model and designing a dataflow architecture that treats memory as a multi-level hierarchy managed explicitly in software. Three key decisions: (1) Reduce the context window from 512 to 64 tokens using a sliding window with learned summarization, cutting KV-cache by 8x. (2) Implement layer-by-layer DMA tiling: only one layer's KV-cache resides in SRAM at a time (~32 KB in INT8), while the next layer is prefetched from Flash via DMA double-buffering. (3) Quantize the KV-cache to INT8, halving per-layer transfer size and fitting within the 1ms latency budget when combined with a reduced 4-layer model. The final SRAM footprint is ~128 KB (32 KB active cache + 32 KB DMA buffer + 64 KB for activations and weights), leaving headroom for the RTOS and sensor buffers.

fixes.scenario: You are the Staff ML Systems Engineer at a company building industrial monitoring devices. Your product uses a single Cortex-M7 MCU (480 MHz, 512 KB SRAM) to run three models on three different sensor streams: (1) Vibration anomaly detection: 60 KB model, 5ms execution time, hard deadline every 10ms (100 Hz vibration sampling). (2) Audio classification: 120 KB model, 50ms execution time, soft deadline every 100ms. (3) Temperature trend prediction: 30 KB model, 2ms execution time, deadline every 1000ms. The v1 firmware runs all three models in a simple round-robin loop. During testing, you discover the vibration model misses its 10ms deadline whenever the audio model is mid-inference. Design a scheduling architecture that guarantees all deadlines are met.
fixes.napkin_math: 1. **Prove v1 Failure (Round-Robin):** Worst case: audio model starts at t=0 (50ms execution). Vibration trigger arrives at t=1ms. Next vibration slot: t=50ms (after audio completes). Deadline miss: 50ms - 10ms = 40ms late. Utilization: U = 5/10 + 50/100 + 2/1000 = 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.002 = 1.002. By Rate Monotonic Analysis, U > 1.0 means the system is not schedulable even with optimal fixed-priority preemption. 2. **Fix with Preemptive Priority + Checkpointing:** Assign priorities: vibration (highest, hard real-time), temperature (medium), audio (lowest, soft). Vibration preempts audio every 10ms for 5ms. Audio effective throughput: 50ms of work spread across 5ms idle windows = 100ms total. Meets its 100ms soft deadline. 3. **Optimization — Chunked Audio Inference:** Split audio model into 5 chunks of 10ms each. Between chunks, vibration can run without context-switch overhead. Vibration worst-case latency: 10ms (1 audio chunk) + 5ms = 15ms. Still exceeds 10ms deadline. Better: split into 10 chunks of 5ms. Vibration worst-case: 5ms (1 chunk) + 5ms = 10ms. Meets deadline exactly. 4. **Memory:** All models in Flash (XIP): 60+120+30 = 210 KB. SRAM for activations: largest is audio at ~45 KB. Vibration checkpoint state: ~8 KB. Total SRAM: ~60 KB of 512 KB = 12%.

Model: 500 KB weights, 20 layers. Small layers (weights < 16 KB): 15 layers, 150 KB total, D-cache hit rate ~95%. Large layers (weights > 16 KB): 5 layers, 350 KB total, D-cache hit rate ~20%. Flash XIP inference: small layers at ~2ns effective + large layers at ~10ns effective. Blended: 15 layers x 2ms + 5 layers x 8ms = 70ms. Selective SRAM copy (large layers only): copy 350 KB to SRAM, then 15 layers x 2ms + 5 layers x 1.5ms = 37.5ms. Speedup: 70 / 37.5 = **1.87x**. SRAM cost: 350 KB (of 1 MB) = 35%. Full SRAM copy: all layers at ~2ns -> 15 x 2ms + 5 x 1.5ms = 37.5ms (same speedup, but costs 500 KB SRAM = 50%). Selective copy gives the same speedup at 30% less SRAM cost (350 KB vs 500 KB).

SRAM layout exploits cascading triggers — only VAD runs continuously; KWS and CMD activate sequentially. Activation arenas: VAD = 8 KB, KWS = 45 KB, CMD = 60 KB. Since KWS and CMD never run simultaneously, share one arena: max(45, 60) = 60 KB. Total SRAM for activations: VAD arena (always resident) + shared KWS/CMD arena = 8 + 60 = 68 KB. If all three arenas were separate: 8 + 45 + 60 = 113 KB. Shared saves 45 KB (40% reduction). Overhead: DMA descriptors, RTOS stack, sensor buffers ~ 20 KB. Total SRAM: 68 + 20 = 88 KB / 512 KB = 17%. Leaves 424 KB headroom for a 4th model. Flash usage: 20 + 80 + 120 = 220 KB for weights. On a typical 1 MB flash device: 22% for models, leaving 780 KB for firmware + OTA staging. Power: VAD always-on at 0.5ms/64ms = 0.78% duty cycle. Average current: 0.0078 x 30 mA + 0.9922 x 0.5 mA = 0.73 mA. Battery life (225 mAh CR2032): 225 / 0.73 = 308 hours = 12.8 days.

fixes.scenario: You are deciding whether tensor parallelism is viable across PCIe-connected GPUs versus NVLink-connected GPUs. Tensor parallelism requires an all-reduce of the activation tensor after every transformer layer. For batch=32, seq=2048, hidden=4096 in FP16, compare the per-layer communication time over NVLink 4.0 (450 GB/s unidirectional) vs PCIe Gen5 x16 (63 GB/s unidirectional).
fixes.common_mistake: Assuming PCIe is 'only a little slower.' The ~7x bandwidth gap makes tensor parallelism over PCIe impractical for latency-sensitive inference, adding milliseconds of overhead per layer.
fixes.realistic_solution: Activation tensor = 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2 bytes = 512 MB. NVLink: 512 MB / 450 GB/s = 1.14 ms. PCIe Gen5: 512 MB / 63 GB/s = 8.1 ms. Over a 32-layer model, PCIe adds 32 * 8.1 = 260 ms vs NVLink's 36 ms. PCIe tensor parallelism adds 7x more communication overhead — this is why tensor parallelism requires NVLink.
fixes.napkin_math: Activation = 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2 = 512 MB. NVLink time = 512 / 450,000 = 1.14 ms. PCIe Gen5 time = 512 / 63,000 = 8.1 ms. Ratio: 7.1x slower on PCIe. Over 32 layers: NVLink total = 36 ms, PCIe total = 260 ms.

fixes.realistic_solution: Phase 1 (0-15ms): Run detection (1.8 GB, 15ms) and path planning (0.5 GB, 8ms) and gesture (0.6 GB, 12ms) concurrently on GPU = 2.9 GB weights + ~4 GB activations = ~7 GB. Phase 2 (15-50ms): SLAM (3.2 GB, 40ms) and speech (0.8 GB, 25ms) concurrently = 4.0 GB weights + ~5 GB activations = ~9 GB. Peak memory: max(7, 9) + 2 GB system overhead = 11 GB — fits in 32 GB. Total wall time: 15 + 40 = 55ms > 50ms deadline. Must optimize: overlap Phase 1 tail with Phase 2 start (speech can begin at t=12ms when gesture finishes), or reduce SLAM to 35ms via TensorRT optimization. Revised: Phase 1 (0-15ms): detection + path + gesture. Phase 2 (12-47ms): speech starts at t=12, SLAM starts at t=15, both finish by t=47ms. Total = 47ms < 50ms. Adding a 6th model (4 GB, 20ms): schedule alongside path planning and gesture in Phase 1, move detection to Phase 2. Phase 1 = max(20, 8, 12) = 20ms, Phase 2 = max(40, 25, 15) = 40ms. Total = 60ms > 50ms. Need to optimize SLAM to < 30ms or pipeline across frames.
fixes.napkin_math: 32 GB LPDDR5 total. 5 models: 3.2+1.8+0.5+0.8+0.6 = 6.9 GB weights. Activations ~ 1.5x weights = 10.4 GB. Peak if all concurrent: 6.9+10.4+2 (system) = 19.3 GB — fits, but deadline violated (SLAM alone = 40ms). Two-phase schedule: Phase 1 = 15ms, Phase 2 = 40ms, total = 55ms. Exceeds 50ms by 5ms. Fix: overlap speech (starts at t=12ms after gesture) with Phase 2 SLAM (starts t=15ms). Effective wall time = 15 + max(40-3, 25-3) = 15 + 37 = 52ms. Still tight — apply TensorRT INT8 to SLAM (typically 1.5-2x speedup, reducing to ~25ms): total = 15 + 25 = 40ms. Peak memory per phase: ~11 GB. 6th model pushes total to 60ms — must optimize SLAM by 10ms.

Baseline throughput (no debiasing): ~2000 samples/s. Without debiasing: AUC=0.85, Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)=0.18. With strong adversary (capacity=5M, static penalty=1.0): AUC=0.73 (-12%), DPD=0.02, Throughput=1200 samples/s. With scheduled penalty and reduced adversary (capacity=0.5M): AUC=0.83 (-2%), DPD=0.04, Throughput=1900 samples/s. The optimized approach sacrifices only 2% AUC for a 77% relative improvement in fairness (DPD: 0.18 -> 0.04), meeting the <0.05 DPD constraint. Throughput recovery: lost = 2000 - 1200 = 800 samples/s, recovered = 1900 - 1200 = 700 samples/s, recovery rate = 700/800 = **87.5%** of the lost throughput.

Old Model: 5% CTR * 120s avg watch time per click = 6.0s expected watch time per impression. New Model: 5.7% CTR (a 14% relative increase, matching the scenario) * 55s avg watch time per click (clickbait degrades engagement) = 3.14s expected watch time per impression. Despite a 14% boost in the technical proxy metric (CTR), the business metric (expected watch time per impression) dropped by 48% — explaining the 15% drop in total platform watch-time (partially offset by increased total clicks). The 8% DAU retention drop follows from sustained user dissatisfaction with lower-quality recommendations.

fixes.scenario: You are optimizing the inference of a Large Language Model with a hidden dimension of 8192. During profiling on an NVIDIA A100 (312 TFLOPS FP16, 2.0 TB/s memory bandwidth), you observe very low SM utilization during the feed-forward network (FFN) layers (matrix size: K=8192, N=32768) when serving requests sequentially at a generation batch size of 1 in FP16. Calculate the minimum batch size required to transition this FFN matrix multiplication from a memory-bound to a compute-bound regime.
fixes.realistic_solution: To make the workload compute-bound, the Arithmetic Intensity (AI) must exceed the hardware's ridge point (156 FLOPs/byte for the A100 at FP16). By increasing the batch size, the cost of loading the large weight matrix from HBM is amortized across multiple input tokens, raising the AI to the compute-bound regime at a batch size of approximately 160.

fixes.scenario: Your team is profiling an auto-regressive decoding microservice serving a 20-billion parameter LLM (FP16, 40GB memory footprint) on a single A100 GPU (2 TB/s HBM2e bandwidth). At a batch size of 1, generating 50 tokens per second, the chip's arithmetic logic units (ALUs) consume merely ~0.2W, but overall chip power draw unexpectedly hits 180W. Diagnose why the chip's power consumption is massively disproportionate to the actual FLOPs performed.
fixes.napkin_math: HBM Read Volume: 40 GB/token * 50 tokens/sec = 2 TB/sec (saturating A100 HBM bandwidth). HBM Energy Cost: ~80 picoJoules (pJ) per byte. Power from memory reads = 2e12 bytes/sec * 80e-12 J/byte = 160 Watts. Compute per token: 2 * 20e9 FLOPs = 40 GFLOPs. At 50 tokens/sec = 2 TFLOP/s. Compute Power: 2e12 FLOP/s * 0.1e-12 J/FLOP = 0.2 Watts. Total: ~160W memory + ~0.2W compute + ~20W static/overhead = ~180W. Memory movement consumes **800x** more power than compute. This is why batch size 1 decode is fundamentally memory-energy-bound.
fixes.realistic_solution: Diagnose the issue as extreme energy-per-byte disparity. At batch size 1, every parameter must be loaded from HBM to SRAM for a single multiply-accumulate. Off-chip HBM access costs ~80 pJ/byte, whereas an FP16 MAC operation costs ~0.1 pJ. On an A100 with 2 TB/s bandwidth, a 40 GB model at batch=1 can generate at most ~50 tokens/sec (2 TB/s / 40 GB). At this rate, memory reads alone consume ~160W while compute uses only ~0.2W. To resolve the power bottleneck, increase the batch size (to amortize weight reads over multiple tokens) or quantize weights to INT4/FP8 to significantly reduce the raw bytes fetched.

fixes.scenario: You are debugging a confidently incorrect prediction from a 50-million parameter ResNet model trained on a 1-million image dataset. To identify which training samples most heavily caused this prediction, you plan to use influence functions. You have a cluster of 100 A100 GPUs (each delivering ~19.5 TFLOPS in FP32). Calculate the computational time and memory required to exactly compute the inverse Hessian, and apply the appropriate approximation technique to solve this.
fixes.napkin_math: Full Hessian memory: (50 x 10^6)^2 x 4 bytes = 10 PB. Exact inverse compute: (50 x 10^6)^3 = 1.25 x 10^23 FLOPs. On 100 A100s at 19.5 TFLOPS FP32 each (1.95 x 10^15 FLOP/s total), time = 1.25 x 10^23 / 1.95 x 10^15 = 6.4 x 10^7 seconds (~742 days, over 2 years). IHVP approximation (LiSSA/CG) reduces compute to O(p) per influence query, taking minutes instead of years on a single GPU.

For Llama-2-70B (80 layers, 8 KV heads via GQA, 128 head_dim), FP16 KV cache per sequence at 8192 context = 2 * 80 * 8 * 128 * 8192 * 2 bytes = 2.68 GB per request. INT8 halves this to 1.34 GB per request. On 8x A100-80GB (640 GB total HBM), after loading the 140 GB model (FP16), ~500 GB remains. FP16 capacity: 500 / 2.68 = ~186 concurrent requests. INT8 capacity: 500 / 1.34 = ~373 concurrent requests — nearly doubling throughput as intended. If you isolate the top 1% outlier Key channels and keep them in FP16, the added memory overhead is 0.01 * 1.34 GB = ~13 MB per request. You restore full FP16 accuracy while retaining ~99% of the memory savings.

TP communication needs ~100 GB/s per layer for typical LLM batches. NVLink provides 600 GB/s (sufficient), but IB at 200 Gbps provides only 25 GB/s (a 24x bottleneck vs NVLink). PP only transmits boundary activations per micro-batch (e.g., micro-batch=1, seq=2048, hidden=12288, BF16: 1 * 2048 * 12288 * 2 = ~50 MB), which can be efficiently pipelined over the 25 GB/s IB link (transfer time ~2ms) without stalling compute that takes 10s of ms per stage.

fixes.scenario: Your team operates a stateful LLM chat service (using a 13B parameter model) on a cluster of 16x 8-GPU H100 nodes. To minimize Time To First Token (TTFT) for multi-turn conversations, user sessions are pinned to specific nodes to reuse the KV cache in GPU HBM. Average context length is 4K tokens. During a single-node hardware failure, the load balancer reroutes the node's 2,000 active sessions to surviving nodes. Within seconds, three surviving nodes crash due to Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors, and the P99 TTFT for the entire cluster spikes from 200ms to over 15 seconds. Analyze the root cause of these cascading failures and explain why standard stateless failover mechanisms exacerbate the problem.
fixes.napkin_math: 13B model with GQA (16 KV heads, 128 head_dim, 40 layers). KV cache per session at 4K tokens: 2 * 40 * 16 * 128 * 4096 * 2 bytes = 1.34 GB per session. 2,000 failed sessions = 2,000 * 1.34 GB = 2.68 TB of lost KV state. Each surviving node has 8x H100 (640 GB HBM). After model weights (~26 GB) and existing sessions, assume ~200 GB free per node across 15 surviving nodes. Rerouting 2,000 sessions across 15 nodes = ~133 sessions/node = 178 GB of KV cache to rebuild per node. This is close to (or exceeds) the free HBM, and the sessions don't arrive uniformly — hot nodes OOM. Rebuilding requires 2,000 * 4,000 = 8M tokens of immediate prefill compute. If a surviving node processes 200K prefill tokens/sec, it takes 40 seconds per node to reconstruct its share, during which request queues explode and HBM is exhausted by concurrent prefill allocations.

At 0.05 cosine distance threshold: 40% hit rate, 2% false positive rate -> 0.8% of all queries get wrong answers (2% of 40%). At 0.01 threshold: 5% hit rate, 0.1% false positive -> 0.005% wrong answers but 87.5% less cost savings. Two-stage (0.05 threshold + classifier): 35% hit rate, 0.1% false positive -> best of both worlds. Cost model: assume $0.003/query for LLM generation. At 10K QPS = 864M queries/month. Naive cache (40% hit): saves 346M queries * $0.003 = $1.04M/month. Two-stage (35% hit): saves 302M queries * $0.003 = $907K/month. Classifier cost: 2ms * 40% of 10K QPS = 4000 calls/s on CPU ~ $500/month. Net savings with two-stage: ~$906K/month with near-zero false positives.

All 70B: 100K * $0.02 = $2,000/day. Naive cascade (sequential, escalate on low confidence): 100K queries all hit 1B ($20). Of those, 40K escalate to 7B ($80). Of those, 15K escalate to 70B ($300). Total: $20 + $80 + $300 = $400/day. Accuracy: the 60K kept by 1B have 85% accuracy; the 25K kept by 7B have 93%; the 15K resolved by 70B have 98%. Effective: (60K*0.85 + 25K*0.93 + 15K*0.98)/100K = 89.0%. With trained router: 65K -> 1B (easy queries, 92% accuracy on these), 20K -> 7B (medium, 95% accuracy), 15K -> 70B (hard, 98% accuracy). Cost: 65K*$0.0002 + 20K*$0.002 + 15K*$0.02 + $5 router = $13 + $40 + $300 + $5 = $358/day. Effective accuracy: (65K*0.92 + 20K*0.95 + 15K*0.98)/100K = 93.5%. To reach 95%: route fewer to 1B (50K at 92%) and more to 7B (35K at 95%) and 70B (15K at 98%): (50K*0.92 + 35K*0.95 + 15K*0.98)/100K = 94.5%. Close — further tuning the router threshold gets to 95%. Cost: $10 + $70 + $300 + $5 = $385/day. Savings vs all-70B: 81%.

Current system: 10K experiments * 10K steps * 200ms = 33 min overhead/experiment * 10K experiments = 5,500 GPU-hours/month wasted on logging. At $3/GPU-hr = $16,500/month. New system: 10K experiments * 10K steps * 1ms = 10 seconds/experiment. Total: 10K * 10s / 3600 = 27.8 GPU-hours/month. Savings: 5,472 GPU-hours/month = $16,417/month. Storage (at current 10K experiments/month): 10K * 10K steps * 10 metrics * 8 bytes = 8 TB/month raw. With columnar compression (10x): 800 GB/month. With 90-day hot retention: 2.4 TB in ClickHouse. Scaling to 100K experiments/month: 80 TB/month raw, 8 TB/month compressed, 24 TB hot. ClickHouse cluster: ~$5K/month. Kafka cluster: ~$3K/month. Total infra: ~$10K/month vs $16.5K/month wasted on logging overhead alone. The system pays for itself.

fixes.realistic_solution: Backward constraint propagation: Step 1 (Serving -> Model Size): P99 < 500ms at 10K QPS on a single GPU. An H100 in INT4 can sustain ~10K-20K tokens/sec for a 7B model. At 10K QPS with average 200 tokens/response: need 2M tokens/sec. A 70B model at BF16 requires ~140 GB — does not fit on a single 80GB GPU, immediately eliminated. A 13B model requires ~26 GB BF16 or ~7 GB INT4, fits on one H100. A 7B model at INT4 (~3.5 GB) fits easily and maximizes throughput headroom. Single GPU constraint -> model must be <= 13B, preferably 7B. Step 2 (Cost -> Precision): $0.001/query at 10K QPS = $10/sec budget. H100 costs ~$3/hr = $0.00083/sec. A single H100 serving INT4 7B at ~15K tokens/sec handles 10K QPS (at ~1.5 tokens/query average for short responses) — cost is $0.00083/sec, well within $10/sec budget. Step 3 (Model -> Training): 7B INT4 serving means training/fine-tuning a 7B BF16 model, then quantizing to INT4 with GPTQ/AWQ. Fine-tuning 7B on domain data: ~10-20 H100-hours. Step 4 (Training -> Data): Fine-tuning on 100K high-quality domain examples. Budget: 100K examples * $0.50/annotation = $50K. The workflow document: a constraint matrix showing which options survive at each stage. Models > 13B eliminated at Stage 1. BF16 serving eliminated at Stage 1 (memory). Training from scratch eliminated at Stage 3 (cost/time).
fixes.napkin_math: Constraint elimination: Start with all model families. After single-GPU memory constraint (80 GB): eliminate > 40B BF16 models. After P99 < 500ms at 10K QPS: eliminate > 13B (insufficient throughput on 1 GPU). After cost constraint: confirms INT4 quantization is optimal (not required, but maximizes margin). After training budget: fine-tune, don't pretrain. Surviving option space: 7B fine-tuned INT4 model on single H100. Key numbers: 7B INT4 = ~3.5 GB VRAM for weights. H100 decode throughput at 7B INT4: ~15-20K tokens/sec. P99 latency for 200-token generation: ~50-100ms (well under 500ms). Development time with constraint propagation: 2-3 weeks. Without (trial-and-error): 2-3 months and $50K+ in wasted compute.

fixes.realistic_solution: Each token: 4096 dims * 2 bytes (BF16) = 8 KB. With top-2 routing, each token is sent to 2 experts. Forward AllToAll: 4096 tokens * 2 routes * 8 KB = 64 MB total dispatched. Return AllToAll: same 64 MB back. Total: 128 MB per MoE layer (both directions). Per-GPU view: each GPU holds 4096/8 = 512 local tokens. Each token routed to 2 experts; with uniform routing, 1/8 of routes stay local, 7/8 go remote. Per-GPU send volume: 512 * 2 * (7/8) * 8 KB = 7 MB. All 8 GPUs send in parallel, so the network must handle 7 MB per GPU concurrently.
fixes.napkin_math: Per token: 4096 * 2B = 8 KB. | Top-2 * 4096 tokens = 8192 token-expert pairs. | Forward dispatch total: 8192 * 8 KB = 64 MB. | Return combine: 64 MB. | Grand total: ~128 MB per layer. | Per-GPU send: 512 tokens * 2 * (7/8) * 8 KB = 7 MB (parallel across all GPUs). | At 200 GB/s NVLink per GPU: 7 MB / 200 GB/s = ~0.035 ms per layer.

30B model, 60 layers, d_head=128, BF16 (2 bytes): | MHA KV per token: 2 * 32 * 128 * 2B * 60 = 960 KB/token. | GQA (8 heads): 2 * 8 * 128 * 2B * 60 = 240 KB/token — 4x smaller. | MQA (1 head): 2 * 1 * 128 * 2B * 60 = 30 KB/token — 32x smaller. | On 80GB A100, model weights ~60GB BF16, leaving ~20GB for KV cache. | Per 4K-context request: MHA = 960 KB * 4096 = 3.84 GB; GQA = 240 KB * 4096 = 0.96 GB; MQA = 30 KB * 4096 = 0.12 GB. | Concurrent 4K requests in 20GB: MHA = 20/3.84 = ~5; GQA = 20/0.96 = ~20; MQA = 20/0.12 = ~166. | GQA delivers 4x the concurrency of MHA with minimal quality loss — the dominant production choice.

Dense 70B: 2x H100, batch 16 (KV cache limited), ~320 tokens/s. Cost per hour = 2 * $3 = $6/hr. Per M tokens: $6/hr / (320 * 3600) * 1e6 = $5.21/M tokens. | MoE 47B: 2x H100, batch 16 (weight memory limited), ~800 tokens/s (5x less compute/token). Per M tokens: $6 / (800 * 3600) * 1e6 = $2.08/M tokens. | SSM 70B-equiv: 2x H100, batch 256+ (no KV cache), ~2000+ tokens/s. Per M tokens: $6 / (2000 * 3600) * 1e6 = $0.83/M tokens. | Ranking: SSM ($0.83/M) < MoE ($2.08/M) < Dense ($5.21/M). SSM wins on throughput (highest batch size from no KV cache), MoE wins on compute efficiency (fewer active FLOPs/token). Dense is most expensive due to full compute + KV cache memory pressure.

Per layer: 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2B = 536,870,912 bytes = 512 MB. 32 layers: 512 MB * 32 = 16 GB activations. Weights (7B, FP16): 14 GB. Adam optimizer states: 2 FP32 copies (momentum + variance) = 7B * 4 bytes * 2 = 56 GB. Gradients (FP16): 14 GB. Total: 14 + 56 + 14 + 16 = 100 GB. This exceeds A100-80GB by 20 GB. The model does NOT fit on a single GPU even before considering activation memory. Solutions: (1) Activation checkpointing reduces activation memory from 16 GB to ~1 GB (recompute during backward). New total: 14 + 56 + 14 + 1 = 85 GB — still over 80 GB. (2) ZeRO Stage 2 (shard optimizer states across GPUs) or mixed-precision training with FP32 master weights is required. Minimum: 2 GPUs with ZeRO-2 + activation checkpointing.
fixes.realistic_solution: Per-layer activation tensor shape: [B, S, H] = [32, 2048, 4096] in FP16 (2 bytes). Per-layer memory: 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2 = 512 MB. Across 32 layers: 512 MB * 32 = 16 GB. Weights: 14 GB. Optimizer states (Adam, FP32 momentum + variance): 7B * 4 bytes * 2 = 56 GB. Gradients: 14 GB. Total: 14 + 56 + 14 + 16 = 100 GB. This does NOT fit on a single A100-80GB. Activation checkpointing is necessary but insufficient alone — ZeRO Stage 2 or model parallelism is also needed to distribute the 56 GB optimizer state.

fixes.napkin_math: Feature importance of 'user_category_affinity': top-5 feature contributing ~8% of model's predictive power. With 40% of users seeing a zeroed feature, the model loses significant signal for those users. Observed conversion drop: 4.2% to 3.1% = 1.1 percentage points absolute, or a 26% relative decline. Revenue impact at $10M monthly revenue: 26% relative conversion drop * $10M = $2.6M/month in lost revenue. Over 3 weeks: ~$2.0M total lost. The fix (correcting the JOIN + backfilling) should restore conversion within 1-2 days as the feature pipeline recovers.

Current P99: 120ms = 25ms model + 95ms feature overhead. Feature overhead decomposition (sequential pipeline): Feature store lookup P99: ~25ms. Real-time feature computation P99: ~40ms. Serialization: ~5ms. Network (2 hops): ~20ms. Sum: 25+40+5+20 = 90ms. Discrepancy: 120 - 25 - 90 = 5ms unaccounted (OS scheduling jitter, GC pauses at tail). Optimization 1 — Parallelize batch + real-time fetch: P99 feature time = max(25, 40) + 5 + 20 = 65ms. Total: 25 + 65 = 90ms < 100ms SLA. Done. Further optimizations if needed: (a) Local feature cache (5s TTL) reduces batch fetch P99 from 25ms to 5ms. (b) Pre-compute real-time features reduces from 40ms to 5ms. After all optimizations: max(5, 5) + 5 + 10 + 25 = 45ms.
fixes.realistic_solution: **Latency decomposition**: (1) Feature store lookup (150 batch features via Redis MGET): ~8ms P50, ~25ms P99 (tail from Redis GC pauses and hot shards). (2) Real-time feature computation (20 features from raw signals): ~15ms P50, ~40ms P99 (Flink window lookups + aggregation). (3) Feature assembly and serialization: ~5ms. (4) Network RTT (2 hops): ~20ms. Current architecture runs these sequentially: P99 = 25 + 40 + 5 + 20 = 90ms features + 25ms model = 115ms. Observed 120ms includes ~5ms of scheduling/GC jitter. **Key optimization — parallelize steps 1 and 2**: fetch batch features and compute real-time features concurrently. New P99: max(25, 40) + 5 + 20 + 25 = 90ms. Meets 100ms SLA. Additional headroom: add local feature cache (5s TTL) for batch features (P99: 25ms -> 5ms) and pre-compute real-time features via Flink push (P99: 40ms -> 5ms). Best case: max(5, 5) + 5 + 10 + 25 = 45ms.

fixes.common_mistake: Trying to serve 2.56 TB of embeddings from GPU HBM for latency. At 10M QPS * 5 tables * 512 bytes/embedding = 25.6 GB/s aggregate read bandwidth — manageable with a distributed system, but far too much data for any single node's memory. This is a distributed systems problem, not a single-machine GPU problem.
fixes.napkin_math: Total read bandwidth: 10M QPS * 5 lookups * 512 bytes = 25.6 GB/s aggregate. L1 hit rate 80%: L1 serves 20.5 GB/s from local RAM (distributed across 200 nodes = ~100 MB/s per node — trivial). L2 hit rate 19%: L2 serves 4.9 GB/s across 16 Redis nodes = ~306 MB/s per node (well within 10 Gbps NIC = 1.25 GB/s). L3 hit rate 1%: L3 serves 256 MB/s across 32 SSD nodes = ~8 MB/s per node (trivial for NVMe). Serving fleet: 200 nodes * $0.50/hr = $100/hr = $876K/yr. The 5ms P99 SLA is achievable because 99% of lookups hit L1/L2 (< 1ms), and only 1% hit SSD-backed L3 (~3-4ms).
